paragraphs: Nam Roi is a Vietnamese pomelo cultivar grown mainly in the Mekong Delta provinces of Vinh Long and Hau Giang. It is prized for being nearly seedless, which made it one of Vietnam's most popular pomelos before newer export varieties rose to prominence.

The fruit is pear-shaped with a yellowish rough rind, and the flesh is pale (white to light yellow). Unlike the bitterness of grapefruit, Nam Roi has a sweet, juicy taste balanced by a mild acidity, with segments that separate easily. It is eaten fresh and also used in pomelo salads.

claims.text: Nam Roi is a Vietnamese pomelo grown mainly in Vinh Long and Hau Giang provinces
It is nearly seedless and was once one of Vietnam's most popular pomelos
Pear-shaped fruit with pale white-to-yellow flesh
Sweet and juicy with a mild acidity, segments separating easily
Eaten fresh and used in pomelo salad

sources.name: Vietnam Online: Vietnamese Pomelo Fruit Types and Benefits
Great Farmer: Vietnamese Pomelo Origins, Characteristics and Varieties

paragraphs: The Tahitian pummelo (Citrus maxima), also recorded as Sarawak pomelo or Moanalua, is believed to have developed in Tahiti from seed brought from Borneo, and was later carried to Hawaii before reaching the UC Riverside collection as budwood in 1971. Unlike the thick, coarse rind of most pomelos, it carries a notably thin, greenish yellow rind on a round fruit slightly flattened at the base, ranging from small to medium-large in size.

Its flesh is greenish to greenish amber, juicy and sweet, with high sugar and moderate acidity. The flavour is distinctive and divisive, often described as melon-like or even lime-like, which some collectors prize and others dislike. It is early to mid-season in maturity and holds well on the tree. Long valued for excellent eating quality, it has stayed a niche variety of local and limited commercial importance.

claims.text: The Tahitian pummelo is believed to have developed in Tahiti from seed from Borneo, then was taken to Hawaii, reaching UC Riverside as budwood in 1971.
It is also known as Sarawak pomelo or Moanalua.
It has a notably thin greenish yellow rind on a round fruit slightly flattened at the base, ranging from small to medium-large.
The flesh is greenish to greenish amber, juicy and sweet, with high sugar and moderate acidity.
The flavour is distinctive and divisive, often described as melon-like or lime-like.
It is early to mid-season in maturity and holds well on the tree, and remains a niche variety of local and limited commercial importance despite excellent eating quality.

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection, UC Riverside: Tahitian pummelo (CRC 3806)
Specialty Produce: Tahitian Pomelos Information and Facts
